• Incyte has reported positive Phase 3 results for tafasitamab (Monjuvi/Minjuvi) in first-line diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, with full data to be presented at the 2026 ASCO Annual Meeting, supporting global regulatory submissions, and separately entered a clinical collaboration with Adagene to test a novel combination therapy in microsatellite stable colorectal cancer in a Phase 1 trial planned for 2026.
  • These advances highlight Incyte’s effort to broaden its oncology portfolio beyond existing products by pairing late-stage assets like tafasitamab with earlier-stage immunotherapy collaborations that could expand its reach across hematologic and solid tumors.

To own Incyte, you need to believe its oncology and immunology portfolio can gradually reduce reliance on Jakafi while supporting solid profitability. The positive Phase 3 tafasitamab data in first-line DLBCL looks like a potential medium term value driver, but it does not change the most immediate swing factor, which remains how investors view the approaching Jakafi patent cliff and the company’s ability to offset it with existing launches and later stage assets.

Among the recent developments, the Phase 3 success of tafasitamab in first-line DLBCL stands out as most relevant. It reinforces the idea that late stage oncology assets could become more meaningful contributors at a time when some analysts expect both revenue and earnings to contract over the next three years. How regulators respond to the upcoming global submissions around tafasitamab may influence how much confidence the market places in Incyte’s diversification story.

data.title: Is Septerna (SEPN) Quietly Redefining Its GPCR Strategy With This First-in-Human SEP-479 Trial?
data.description:
• Septerna, Inc. recently began dosing healthy volunteers in a randomized, placebo-controlled Phase 1 trial of SEP-479, an oral PTH1R agonist for hypoparathyroidism, using single-ascending and multiple-ascending dose designs to assess safety, tolerability, pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics.
  • This first-in-human study, which could enroll up to 150 participants and tracks biomarkers such as endogenous PTH and serum calcium, marks a key progression of Septerna’s GPCR-targeted pipeline into clinical testing.
  • With early clinical progress on SEP-479 now underway, we’ll examine how this first-in-human trial shapes Septerna’s broader investment narrative.

Find 63 companies with promising cash flow potential yet trading below their fair value.

Advertisement

What Is Septerna's Investment Narrative?

For Septerna, you have to believe in its GPCR platform turning early science into real medicines, not just partnerships. The new SEP-479 first-in-human trial nudges that story forward by adding a second wholly owned program in the clinic, alongside SEP-631, and gives the pipeline more depth behind the Novo Nordisk collaboration. In the near term, the key catalysts still look like SEP-631’s planned Phase 2b start in chronic spontaneous urticaria and any updates on the Novo partnership economics, with the SEP-479 readout sitting further out in 2026 or 2027. The immediate impact of the SEP-479 news on those near-term drivers is limited, but it modestly de-risks the platform narrative while also reinforcing the biggest current risks: high cash burn, a premium valuation and clinical execution across multiple early programs.

However, one less obvious risk could catch shareholders off guard if things change suddenly. Our valuation report here indicates Septerna may be overvalued.

Exploring Other Perspectives

Two fair value estimates from the Simply Wall St Community span an extremely wide range, underlining how differently people see Septerna’s worth.   • The case raises questions about workplace practices and competition issues that could carry legal and reputational implications for the wider LVMH group.

LVMH, through ENXTPA:MC, is a central player in the global luxury goods market, with brands across fashion, leather goods, wines, spirits, perfumes, cosmetics, watches, and jewelry. In this context, allegations around workplace treatment and pricing conduct at Stella McCartney bring non financial risk factors into sharper focus for readers following the group.

For investors tracking ENXTPA:MC, this lawsuit becomes part of a broader picture that includes regulatory interest in Europe and growing attention to environmental, social, and governance issues.
